title: SEGMENT 4 — Intestinal organoids and the rise of personalized cancer medicine

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Intestinal organoids and the rise of personalized cancer medicine
The intestinal organoid was the original, and it remains, in many ways, the most useful and most extensively developed of all organoid systems.
Let me give you a sense of how the field has built on Sato and Clevers' 2009 paper over the subsequent decade and a half.
The first big extension was to human tissue.
The original 2009 paper used mouse intestinal cells.
By 2011, the same group had adapted the protocol to grow organoids from human gut biopsies.
The recipe needed tweaking — more Wnt, slightly different growth factors — but the basic principle held.

You could take a tiny pinch of tissue from a colonoscopy, dissociate it, drop it in Matrigel with the right cocktail, and within a few weeks you'd have human intestinal organoids growing in your dish, derived from that specific patient.
This opened up a whole new mode of clinical research.
You could now study, in dishes, the gut biology of specific people.
You could compare organoids from healthy donors to organoids from patients with inflammatory bowel disease.

You could derive organoids from patients with cystic fibrosis — whose gut epithelia carry the disease-causing mutation in the CFTR chloride channel — and use those organoids to test which CFTR-modulator drugs would actually work on that patient's particular CFTR variant.

There's a beautiful little assay called the forskolin swelling assay: you treat the organoid with forskolin, which activates CFTR, which pumps chloride into the lumen, which draws in water by osmosis, which causes the organoid to swell up like a balloon if the channel is working.
If you treat a cystic fibrosis patient's organoid with a candidate drug and then with forskolin, the amount of swelling tells you whether the drug rescued that patient's CFTR function.

In the Netherlands, organoid-based forskolin swelling assays are now used to direct cystic fibrosis treatment decisions for patients with rare CFTR mutations that the standard clinical trials never included.
But the real takeoff, the application that brought organoids into the clinical mainstream, was tumor organoids.
Here's the idea.
Cancer is, at its heart, a disease of bad cell behavior.
Tumor cells grow when they shouldn't, ignore signals to die, invade tissues they shouldn't be in.
For decades, we've tried to develop drugs that fix this — and we've had some success — but cancer is heterogeneous.
Two patients with what looks like the same cancer can respond very differently to the same drug.
The reason is that, genetically and biochemically, their tumors are different.
One patient's lung cancer has mutations in EGFR; another's has mutations in KRAS; another's has none of those but some other driver entirely.
The right drug for one patient may be useless for another.
What if you could test each patient's tumor, individually, in a dish, before deciding what drug to give them?
This is what tumor organoids enable.

The recipe is essentially the Sato and Clevers protocol with some modifications: you take a piece of a patient's tumor — from surgery, or from a needle biopsy — dissociate the tumor cells, embed them in Matrigel, and feed them a cocktail of growth factors.
Tumor cells, it turns out, are perfectly happy to form organoids.
In fact, because they're already dysregulated for proliferation, they often grow more easily than their normal counterparts.

Within a few weeks, you have organoids that carry the genetic alterations of the original tumor, that grow at roughly the same rate as the original tumor, and that respond to drugs in a way that, in most cases, predicts how the original tumor would respond.
The Marc van de Wetering and Hans Clevers group at the Hubrecht has been at the forefront of this work.
They've built a biobank of patient-derived colorectal cancer organoids — hundreds of different patient tumors, all preserved, all characterized, all available for drug screening.
They've shown that you can take a patient's tumor organoids, screen them against a panel of 80 or so different cancer drugs, and identify which drugs kill that patient's organoids best.
And in clinical trials, those organoid-screening results are starting to predict patient outcomes.
Picture what this means practically.
Take a piece of your tumor.
Grow it as an organoid.
Test a hundred drugs in parallel.
Treat you with whichever one shrinks the dish.
That's the dream of personalized cancer medicine in one sentence, and tumor organoids make it possible.

Not yet for every cancer — some tumors don't grow well as organoids, some patients can't wait the few weeks it takes to establish a culture, some drug responses don't translate cleanly — but the proof of concept is there, and the technology is getting faster and more predictive every year.
There are now organoid biobanks for colorectal cancer, breast cancer, pancreatic cancer, prostate cancer, lung cancer, liver cancer, bladder cancer, and more.
Each biobank is a repository of patient-derived tumors that are, essentially, replayable.

Researchers who want to study why a particular drug works in some patients but not others can take the existing biobank, find tumors that responded and tumors that didn't, and ask what's different about them.
The biobank is, in effect, a library of patient avatars — each one a tiny biological replica of a real person's cancer, available for experimentation in ways the real patient never could be.
The Hubrecht's biobank alone now contains organoids from thousands of patients across many cancer types.
It's a public resource — academic labs can request access for research — and it's already enabled studies that would have been impossible by any other means.
You can ask: of all the breast cancers in the biobank, which ones respond to drug X?
And then look at the genetics of those tumors and try to find the signature that predicts response.
That signature, validated, becomes a clinical biomarker.
There's a lovely conceptual point hidden in the tumor organoid story.
Cancer organoids work because cancer is, in some ways, a hijacking of normal stem cell biology.
Many tumors contain a population of cells with stem-like properties — they self-renew, they generate the bulk tumor cells, they resist treatment.
Those cancer stem cells respond to the same signaling pathways — Wnt, R-spondin, EGF, the rest — that intestinal stem cells respond to.
So the very cocktail Sato developed for normal intestinal organoids works, often with minor modifications, for intestinal tumor organoids.
Cancer didn't invent new biology; it corrupted existing biology.
And the existing biology, once we understand it, gives us the tools to study cancer in dishes.
The bigger lesson: once you have the right culture system for a tissue, you often get the cancer of that tissue as a bonus.
Liver organoids led naturally to liver cancer organoids.
Pancreatic organoids led to pancreatic cancer organoids.
The technology generalizes, and so the personalized-medicine approach generalizes too.
We'll come back to this in a later segment when we talk about the future of clinical organoid use.
For now, let's move on to the next major organ system that surrendered to the organoid approach.
